label: fiction
title: The Cellar Print
charge: Burglary — breaking into a jeweller's strongroom
elements: The strongroom was broken into and its contents taken.

beats.text: The defence expert is compelling: fingerprint identification has failed before. He recites documented misattributions, examiner bias, certainty overstated on oath. 'This discipline has convicted innocent men.'
beats.reveal_note: Every word true — of other cases. Generic doubt about a discipline is not a defect in this evidence, which was verified blind and stands beside blood and stolen goods.

beats.text: The broken window sash yielded blood, dried into the splintered frame. The profile matches the accused.
beats.reveal_note: The borrowed scepticism has nothing to say to this: his blood, his cut, his window.

beats.text: Bearer bonds from the strongroom, recovered from the chimney flue of the accused's flat.
beats.reveal_note: Possession of the proceeds, hidden — the beat no cross-examination touched.

beats.text: The fingerprint examiner: three prints on the strongroom door, verified independently by a second examiner who was told nothing about the case or the suspect.
beats.reveal_note: The blind check is exactly the safeguard whose absence the defence expert lamented — and it was present here.

beats.text: The judge: scepticism is a tool for weighing evidence, not a verdict. The jury must ask what this evidence shows — not what other evidence, in other courts, once failed to.
beats.reveal_note: The hard lesson inverted: warnings about junk science can be aimed at sound science too. Doubt is owed to the evidence in front of you, not borrowed from history.

twist: Everything the defence said about forensic failure was true — somewhere else. Here there were three blind-verified prints, his blood on the sash, and the bonds up his own chimney. Scepticism is a tool; his counsel used it as a smoke machine.
